The '''Kuwait national football team''' ({{langx|ar|منتخب الكويت لكرة القدم|translit=muntakhab alkuayt likurat alqadam}}) represents [[Kuwait]] in international [[Association football|football]] and it is controlled by the [[Kuwait Football Association]]. Kuwait made its only [[FIFA World Cup]] appearance in [[1982 FIFA World Cup|1982]] and managed to get one draw in the group stage against [[Czechoslovakia national football team|Czechoslovakia]]. In the [[AFC Asian Cup]], [[Kuwait]] reached the final in [[1976 AFC Asian Cup|1976]] and won the tournament four years later in [[1980 AFC Asian Cup|1980]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Asia's greatest national teams: Kuwait (1980s) {{!}} Football News {{!}} Asian Qualifiers 2022|url=https://www.the-afc.com/competitions/afc-asian-cup/news/asia-s-greatest-national-teams-kuwait-1980s|access-date=2020-06-09|website=AFC|language=en-GB|archive-date=2020-06-09|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200609051221/https://www.the-afc.com/competitions/afc-asian-cup/news/asia-s-greatest-national-teams-kuwait-1980s|url-status=live}}</ref>

The Kuwait national football team has faced multiple suspensions by [[FIFA]] due to governmental interference and failure to comply with regulations, notably in 2007, 2008, and 2015. These suspensions, which were later lifted after legal reforms, caused the team to miss major tournaments, including the [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|2018 FIFA World Cup]] and [[2019 AFC Asian Cup qualification|2019 AFC Asian Cup qualifiers]], and led to a significant drop in the country's [[FIFA Men's World Ranking|FIFA World Rankings]]. In 2017, following the lifting of its suspension, Kuwait hosted the [[23rd Arabian Gulf Cup|Gulf Cup]].

==History==
===Early successes===
Kuwait's first international match at the [[1961 Arab Games]] against [[Libya national football team|Libya]] ended in a 2–2 draw. Their main losses were 8–0 defeats to both the [[Egypt national football team|United Arab Republic]] and [[Portugal national football team|Portugal]]. Kuwait hosted the [[3rd Arabian Gulf Cup|1974 Arabian Gulf Cup]] at the [[Al Kuwait Sports Club Stadium]], collecting their third consecutive title in the cup. They won the [[AFC Asian Cup]] in [[1980 AFC Asian Cup|1980]]. Kuwait's national football team joined the World Cup in [[1982 FIFA World Cup|1982]], which was held in [[Spain]]. [[Kuwait]] was placed in Group 4 where it lost against [[England national football team|England]] and [[France national football team|France]] and managed a draw against [[Czechoslovakia]]. Kuwait's highest-ever [[FIFA ranking]] was 24th place, achieved in December 1998.

[[Bader Al-Mutawa]] is the most capped player on the Kuwaiti team, [[Bashar Abdullah]] is their top scorer. Kuwait has won the [[Arabian Gulf Cup]] ten times and is the most successful team in winning the competition. Kuwait's most successful manager was [[Luiz Felipe Scolari]], who had previously won the FIFA World Cup with [[Brazil national football team|Brazil]]. Scolari led Kuwait to win the [[10th Arabian Gulf Cup|1990 Arabian Gulf Cup]], beating [[Qatar national football team|Qatar]] in the final. He was then forced to leave the country after the [[Iraqi invasion of Kuwait|1990 invasion by Iraq]].

Kuwait's largest victory was a 20–0 win against [[Bhutan national football team|Bhutan]] on 14 February 2000. At the time, it was the highest margin of victory in international football until [[Australia men's national soccer team|Australia]] defeated [[American Samoa national football team|American Samoa]] 31–0 on [[Australia 31–0 American Samoa|11 April 2001]].

===Impact of the Iraqi Invasion===
The [[Iraqi invasion of Kuwait]] in August 1990 significantly affected the Kuwaiti national football team. During the occupation, sports activities were severely disrupted as many athletes, including football players, were displaced or went into exile. The Kuwait Football Association's headquarters and facilities were damaged, leading to the suspension of all football competitions in the country. Consequently, the national team had to miss the [[1992 AFC Asian Cup qualification|1992 AFC Asian Cup qualifiers]].

One of the most tragic incidents during the Iraqi invasion of Kuwait was the death of Sheikh [[Fahad Al-Ahmed Al-Jaber Al-Sabah]], who was killed [[Battle of Dasman Palace|defending the palace]]. on 2 August 1990.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1990-08-04-mn-600-story.html|title=Kuwait Prince Killed in Palace Battle|work=Los Angeles Times|date=4 August 1990|access-date=15 April 2025}}</ref> He was a prominent figure in Kuwaiti sports, serving as President of the [[Kuwait Football Association]] (1978–1990) and a member of the [[International Olympic Committee]] (1981–1990). Under his leadership, Kuwait won the [[1980 AFC Asian Cup]] and qualified for the [[1982 FIFA World Cup]], becoming the first Asian Arab nation to do so.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.the-afc.com/competitions/afc-asian-cup/|title=1980 AFC Asian Cup Results|publisher=AFC|access-date=15 April 2025}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.fifa.com/tournaments/mens/worldcup/1982spain/teams/43922|title=Kuwait at the 1982 FIFA World Cup|publisher=FIFA|access-date=15 April 2025}}</ref> His death was a severe loss to Kuwaiti sports, and his legacy is honored through various tournaments and stadiums named after him.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.upi.com/Archives/1990/08/06/Arab-sports-world-loses-leader/1354649915200/|title=Arab sports world loses leader|publisher=UPI|date=6 August 1990|access-date=15 April 2025}}</ref>

Despite these challenges, the Kuwaiti national football team participated in the [[1990 Asian Games]] held in Beijing, China, from 23 September to 6 October 1990. The participation was organized by the exiled Kuwaiti Olympic Committee, operating from Saudi Arabia, and involved athletes who were already abroad during the invasion. According to a report by the Los Angeles Times dated 17 September 1990, 42 Kuwaiti athletes, including 23 football players, arrived in Beijing to represent Kuwait in the tournament.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1990-09-17-sp-724-story.html|title=Exiled Kuwaitis Arrive in China|work=Los Angeles Times|date=17 September 1990|access-date=15 April 2025}}</ref>

Kuwait was placed in Group C alongside Thailand, Yemen, and Hong Kong. The team finished seventh overall, with one win, one draw, and two losses, scoring three goals and conceding four.

====Group C====
{{#invoke:sports table|main|style=WDL
|winpoints=2
|team1=THA|name_THA={{fb|THA}}
|team2=KUW|name_KUW={{fb|KUW}}
|team3=HKG|name_HKG={{fb|HKG|1959}}
|team4=YEM|name_YEM={{fb|YEM}}
|win_THA=2|draw_THA=1|loss_THA=0|gf_THA=4|ga_THA=1
|win_KUW=1|draw_KUW=1|loss_KUW=1|gf_KUW=3|ga_KUW=3
|win_HKG=1|draw_HKG=0|loss_HKG=2|gf_HKG=3|ga_HKG=4
|win_YEM=0|draw_YEM=2|loss_YEM=1|gf_YEM=0|ga_YEM=2
|col_A=#ccffcc
|result1=A|result2=A
|update=complete|source=[https://www.rsssf.org/tablesa/asgames90.html RSSSF]
}}

Kuwait's participation in the [[1990 Asian Games]] came just weeks after the Iraqi invasion, with athletes competing under exceptional circumstances and limited resources, marking one of the most challenging moments in the nation's sporting history.

Kuwait's football league, the [[Kuwait Premier League|Kuwaiti Premier League]], was also suspended, leading to a significant loss of competitive match experience for players. Many training facilities and stadiums were either damaged or repurposed for military use during the invasion, further hampering the team's preparations and development. Additionally, financial constraints post-invasion led to reduced funding for sports, affecting the quality of training and support available to athletes.

In the years that followed these challenges, the team achieved steady and measurable progress. In 1996, Kuwait won the [[Arabian Gulf Cup]], showcasing its resilience and ability to recover from the invasion's setbacks. The national team also qualified for the [[1996 AFC Asian Cup]], advancing to the semi-final, which was a significant achievement given the circumstances. The invasion's long-term effects were felt across the sporting community, highlighting the resilience and determination of Kuwaiti athletes and officials in rebuilding their footballing legacy post-occupation.<ref name="Impact of the Iraqi invasion">{{cite web |title=Impact of the Iraqi invasion on Kuwaiti sports |url=https://www.fifa.com |website=FIFA.com |access-date=10 June 2024}}</ref><ref name="AFC Asian Cup">{{cite web |title=AFC Asian Cup |url=https://www.the-afc.com |website=The-AFC.com |access-date=10 June 2024}}</ref><ref name="How Iraqs invasion">{{cite web |title=How Iraq's invasion affected Kuwait's football |url=https://www.arabianbusiness.com |website=ArabianBusiness.com |access-date=10 June 2024}}</ref>

===Suspensions===
On 30 October 2007, Kuwait was suspended by [[FIFA]] from all participation in international football on the grounds of governmental interference in the national football association.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://football.guardian.co.uk/breakingnews/feedstory/0,,-7035021,00.html|title=FIFA suspends Kuwait Football Federation|publisher=[[Guardian.co.uk]]|access-date=30 October 2007|location=London|archive-date=7 May 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200507233833/http://football.guardian.co.uk/breakingnews/feedstory/0,,-7035021,00.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> However, the ban was short-lived, lasting less than 2 weeks.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.fifa.com/worldfootball/releases/newsid=634716.html#fifa+lifts+suspension+kuwait+conditional+basis|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071111044852/http://www.fifa.com/worldfootball/releases/newsid=634716.html#fifa+lifts+suspension+kuwait+conditional+basis|url-status=dead|archive-date=11 November 2007|title=FIFA lifts suspension on Kuwait|publisher=[[FIFA]]|access-date=9 November 2007}}</ref> On 24 October 2008, Kuwait was again suspended by FIFA from all participation in international football for failing to hold the General Assembly elections by mid-October.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.the-afc.com/eng/articles/viewArticle.jsp_168297446.html |title=FIFA suspends Kuwait Football Federation |publisher=The-AFC.com |access-date=25 October 2008 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081026215317/http://www.the-afc.com/eng/articles/viewArticle.jsp_168297446.html |archive-date=26 October 2008 }}</ref>  FIFA provisionally lifted its suspension on the Kuwait Football Association (KFA) on 22 December 2008.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.the-afc.com/eng/articles/viewArticle.jsp_168395342.html |title=FIFA provisionally lifted its suspension on the Kuwait Football Association (KFA) |publisher=The-AFC.com |access-date=22 December 2008 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081225100331/http://www.the-afc.com/eng/articles/viewArticle.jsp_168395342.html |archive-date=25 December 2008 }}</ref>

Once again on 16 October 2015, Kuwait was suspended for the third time as FIFA did not recognize the new sports law in the country.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.fifa.com/governance/news/y=2015/m=10/news=suspension-of-the-kuwait-football-association-2717726.html?intcmp=fifacom_hp_module_about_fifa|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160109031714/http://www.fifa.com/governance/news/y=2015/m=10/news=suspension-of-the-kuwait-football-association-2717726.html?intcmp=fifacom_hp_module_about_fifa|url-status=dead|archive-date=9 January 2016|title=Suspension of the Kuwait Football Association|publisher=FIFA|access-date=16 October 2015}}</ref> Kuwait tried to get the suspension lifted at the [[FIFA Congress|66th FIFA Congress]], but the proposal was rejected. Therefore, to the earlier announcement on 27 April 2016, the hosting of the Gulf Cup tournament would also be moved to Qatar.<ref>{{cite web|title=FIFA Congress drives football forward, first female secretary general appointed|url=https://inside.fifa.com/about-fifa/organisation/media-releases/fifa-congress-drives-football-forward-first-female-secretary-general-a-2790957|publisher=FIFA|access-date=14 May 2016}}</ref> The suspension was lifted on 6 December 2017, after Kuwait adopted a new sports law.<ref>{{cite news|title=Suspension of the Kuwait Football Association lifted|url=https://inside.fifa.com/legal/football-regulatory/media-releases/suspension-of-the-kuwait-football-association-lifted-2922929|access-date=6 December 2017|publisher=FIFA.com|date=6 December 2017}}</ref> By this time, the team had fallen from 139th place to 189th place in the FIFA World Rankings due to its inactivity, which also caused the team to miss the qualifiers for the [[2018 FIFA World Cup]] and the [[2019 AFC Asian Cup]].<ref name="teamsconfirmed">{{cite web |url=https://www.the-afc.com/en/national/afc_asian_cup/news/teams_for_final_round_of_afc_asian_cup_uae_2019_qualifiers_confirmed.html|title=Teams for final round of AFC Asian Cup UAE 2019 qualifiers confirmed |publisher=Asian Football Confederation |access-date=13 January 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=The FIFA/Coca-Cola World Ranking - November 2017|url=https://www.fifa.com/fifa-world-ranking/ranking-table/men/rank=280/afc.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171125205143/http://www.fifa.com/fifa-world-ranking/ranking-table/men/rank=280/afc.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=25 November 2017|website=FIFA.com|access-date=6 December 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web| url = https://www.arabianbusiness.com/gcc/kuwait/385052-fifa-lifts-suspension-on-kuwaits-football-association|title = FIFA lifts suspension on Kuwait's football association - Kuwait, Europe - ArabianBusiness.com| date=6 December 2017 }}</ref>

On 7 December 2017, it was announced that Kuwait would host the [[23rd Arabian Gulf Cup|2017 Gulf Cup]] tournament after [[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi Arabia]], the [[United Arab Emirates national football team|United Arab Emirates]], and [[Bahrain national football team|Bahrain]] had all withdrawn. The tournament was previously set to be hosted by Qatar, but, because of the [[2017–18 Qatar diplomatic crisis|Qatari diplomatic crisis]], it was moved to Kuwait, and the withdrawn nations joined again.

===Revival===
During the [[2022 FIFA World Cup qualification]], Kuwait, which only started to rebuild its team following years of suspensions, was drawn into Group B alongside [[Australia men's national soccer team|Australia]], Jordan, Nepal, and Chinese Taipei. Kuwait's performance in the qualifiers reflected the impact of previous suspensions and instabilities, as the team lost 0–3 twice. Still, the qualifiers stood out as the best qualification for Kuwait since 2006, as Kuwait finished second and was unable to progress to the third round.

In June 2022, Kuwait hosted the third round of the [[2023 AFC Asian Cup]] qualifiers. Kuwait failed to qualify, as they lost to Indonesia and Jordan.

In June 2023, Kuwait was invited as a guest team in the [[2023 SAFF Championship]], where it finished as runner-up, losing to India during a penalty shootout in the final.

In June 2024, Kuwait qualified for the [[2027 AFC Asian Cup]] after nine years of absence and advanced into the third round of the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC third round|2026 FIFA World Cup qualification]].

After the controversial match against [[Iraq national football team|Iraq]], which ended in a goalless draw, the Kuwait Football Association board resigned on 17 September 2024 due to chaotic scenes where fans suffered in extreme temperatures and some ticket holders were denied entry. The Kuwaiti FA launched an inquiry and subsequently approved the resignations of several key officials. Following the backlash, the KFA suspended its Secretary-General, Salah Al-Qanai, and Public Relations Director, Mohamed Bou Abbas. Dr. Saleh Al-Majroub was later appointed as acting Secretary-General to lead the federation during the interim period.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.middleeastmonitor.com/20240916-kuwait-fa-board-resigns-after-world-cup-qualifier-fiasco/|title=Kuwait FA board resigns after World Cup qualifier fiasco|publisher=Middle East Monitor|date=16 September 2024|access-date=15 April 2025}}</ref>

Following that match, Kuwait entered a period of fluctuating results. On 10 October 2024, they suffered a heavy 0–4 defeat to [[Oman national football team|Oman]] in Muscat,<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/soccer/match/_/gameId/710455/kuwait-oman|title=Match report: Kuwait vs Oman|work=ESPN|access-date=15 April 2025}}</ref> followed by a 2–2 draw against [[Palestine national football team|Palestine]] in Doha on 15 October. The team then hosted [[South Korea national football team|South Korea]] on 14 November and lost 1–3,<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/soccer/match/_/gameId/699999/kuwait-south-korea|title=Match report: Kuwait vs South Korea|work=ESPN|access-date=15 April 2025}}</ref> before securing a 1–1 draw with [[Jordan national football team|Jordan]] on 19 November in Kuwait City. In December, Kuwait played three friendlies in Doha: a 1–1 draw with [[Yemen national football team|Yemen]] on the 9th, and two consecutive defeats to [[Lebanon national football team|Lebanon]] on the 12th and 15th (1–2 and 0–2 respectively).

During the [[26th Arabian Gulf Cup]] hosted in Kuwait from 21 to 31 December 2024, the national team aimed to revive its historical status as the tournament's most successful side with ten titles. The competition began with a 1–1 draw against [[Oman national football team|Oman]], followed by an impressive 2–1 victory over the [[United Arab Emirates national football team|United Arab Emirates]]. In their final group stage match, Kuwait drew 1–1 with [[Qatar national football team|Qatar]], securing qualification to the semi-finals as group runners-up. The semi-final match against [[Bahrain national football team|Bahrain]] was a closely contested encounter. Despite creating several chances, Kuwait lost 0–1, ending their hopes of winning an eleventh title on home soil.

The tournament marked Kuwait's return as a competitive force in the region, with solid performances that drew positive attention from local media. Sports analysts and newspapers such as ''Al Rai'' and ''Al Qabas'' praised the team's fighting spirit and improved organization under interim leadership, despite not reaching the final.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.alraimedia.com/article/1653124/رياضة/رياضة-محلية/إشادة-بالأزرق-رغم-الخروج|title=إشادة بالأزرق رغم الخروج|publisher=Al Rai|date=1 January 2025|access-date=15 April 2025}}</ref> There was also renewed public enthusiasm, with several matches drawing near-capacity crowds at Jaber Al-Ahmad International Stadium. However, some criticism remained regarding the team's lack of clinical finishing, and calls were made for continued reforms in youth development and coaching infrastructure.

In 2025, the Kuwait national football team continued to experience inconsistent performances. On 20 March, Kuwait played against [[Iraq national football team|Iraq]] in Basra, where the match ended in a 2–2 draw. Yousef Nasser scored both goals for Kuwait, while Iraq managed to equalize with two late goals in stoppage time through Akam Hashim and Ibrahim Bayesh.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.espn.com/soccer/match/_/gameId/710490/kuwait-iraq|title=Match Report: Kuwait 2–2 Iraq|work=ESPN|access-date=15 April 2025}}</ref>

Five days later, on 25 March, Kuwait hosted [[Oman national football team|Oman]] at Jaber Al-Ahmad International Stadium but suffered a narrow 0–1 defeat, with Issam Al-Subhi scoring the decisive goal for Oman.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.espn.com/soccer/match/_/gameId/710503/oman-kuwait|title=Match Report: Kuwait 0–1 Oman|work=ESPN|access-date=15 April 2025}}</ref> After that match, Kuwait's qualifiers ended with a 2-0 and 4–0 defeat to [[Palestine national football team|Palestine]] at home and [[South Korea national football team|South Korea]] away respectively.

In November 2025, Kuwait defeated [[Mauritania national football team|Mauritania]] in the [[2025 FIFA Arab Cup qualification|2025 FIFA Arab Cup Qualifiers]] and Qualified to the Finals of the [[2025 FIFA Arab Cup]], the first time since the [[2012 Arab Cup]].

==Team image==

=== Kit and emblem ===
The Kuwait national football team is recognized for its blue kits while playing at home, symbolizing the nation's maritime heritage and the blue of the Arabian Gulf. The team's emblem features a falcon, a national symbol of Kuwait.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Embassy of the State of Kuwait in Japan |url=https://www.kuwait-embassy.or.jp/E_outline_07.html#:~:text=The%20present%20emblem%20of%20Kuwait,on%20blue%20and%20white%20waves. |access-date=2024-09-10 |website=www.kuwait-embassy.or.jp}}</ref> [[Adidas]] is acting as Kuwait's official kit provider since 2023.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kuwait Kit History |url=https://www.footballkitarchive.com/kuwait-kits/ |access-date=2024-09-10 |website=Football Kit Archive |language=en-US}}</ref>

===Stadium===

[[File:Kazmastadium.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Al-Sadaqua Walsalam Stadium]]
The Kuwait national football team primarily plays its home matches at the Jaber Al-Ahmad International Stadium, a modern multi-purpose venue inaugurated in 2009 with a capacity of over 60,000 spectators.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.goalzz.com/main.aspx?showstadiums=1&stadium=4376|title=Jaber Al-Ahmad International Stadium|publisher=Goalzz|access-date=15 April 2025}}</ref> This stadium has hosted major international fixtures, including Gulf Cup matches and Asian qualifiers.

Before its completion, the national team played most of its home matches at Mohammed Al-Hamad Stadium in Hawalli, a smaller venue traditionally associated with Al-Qadsia SC. Kuwait has also used other stadiums on occasion, such as Al-Sadaqua Walsalam Stadium, home of Kazma SC, and Ali Al-Salem Al-Sabah Stadium, home of Al-Nasr SC in Farwaniya. These alternative venues are typically selected based on logistical needs or specific tournament requirements.

In recent years, the newly inaugurated [[Sulaibikhat Stadium]] in [[Sulaibikhat]], has been added to Kuwait's list of modern football venues. Opened in December 2024, it features a European-style football-specific design without an athletics track and has a capacity of 15,000 spectators. It serves as the home ground for Sulaibikhat SC and has also hosted select matches for Al-Arabi SC.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.alraimedia.com/article/1651842/رياضة/الملاعب/افتتاح-استاد-جابر-المبارك-في-الصليبخات|title=افتتاح استاد جابر المبارك في الصليبخات|publisher=Al Rai|date=11 December 2024|access-date=15 April 2025}}</ref>

===Broadcasting===
Kuwait national football team matches are primarily broadcast on Kuwait TV Sport, which provides full match commentary. During major tournaments such as the Arabian Gulf Cup and the AFC Asian Cup, matches are additionally broadcast on beIN Sports and Dubai Sports.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://en.yemenshabab.net/sections/NEWS/Sports/gulf-26-in-kuwait--match-schedule-and-broadcasting-channels|title=Gulf 26 in Kuwait: Match schedule and broadcasting channels|work=يمن شباب نت |publisher=Yemen Shabab|access-date=15 April 2025}}</ref>

== Rivalries ==
;[[Iraq–Kuwait football rivalry|Kuwait vs. Iraq]]
* {{flagicon|Iraq}} '''[[Iraq national football team|Iraq]]'''

{| style="float: right; margin-left: 1em; margin-bottom: 0.5em; width: 200px; border: solid 1px"
|-
|
{| class="wikitable" style="text-align: center;font-size:90%;"
!colspan="6"|Statistics vs. {{fb|Iraq}}<ref>{{Cite web |title=Iraq - Record against... |url=https://www.worldfootball.net/teams/te1059/iraq/records-all-time-appearances/ |access-date=2023-01-11 |website=worldfootball.net |language=en |archive-date=2023-01-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230111005424/https://www.worldfootball.net/teams/irak-team/11/ |url-status=live }}</ref>
|-
!Played<sup>1</sup>
!Wins<sup>2</sup>
!Draws
!Losses
!GF
!GA
|-
|37||8||11||17||36||50
|}
<small>''1. Only matches recognized by [[FIFA]].''</small><br>
<small>''2. Wins for '''Kuwait'''.''</small>
|}
[[Iraq national football team|Iraq]]'s rivalry with Kuwait has been one of the most notable [[football rivalry|football rivalries]] in the Arab world.<ref name="iraq-kuwait">{{Cite web |url=https://www.thenationalnews.com/sport/storied-gulf-cup-rivalry-between-iraq-and-kuwait-survives-war-1.473125/ |title=Storied Gulf Cup rivalry between Iraq and Kuwait survives war |access-date=2013-07-23 |archive-date=2016-08-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160829210322/http://www.thenational.ae/sport/football/storied-gulf-cup-rivalry-between-iraq-and-kuwait-survives-war |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Storied Gulf Cup rivalry between Iraq and Kuwait survives war|url=https://www.thenationalnews.com/sport/storied-gulf-cup-rivalry-between-iraq-and-kuwait-survives-war-1.473125|access-date=2021-08-29|website=The National|date=31 December 2012|archive-date=2022-08-20|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220820062951/https://www.thenationalnews.com/sport/storied-gulf-cup-rivalry-between-iraq-and-kuwait-survives-war-1.473125|url-status=live}}</ref> The rivalry began in the mid-1970s, and it was the decade from 1976 until 1986 that saw the golden age of football for arguably the finest teams the region has produced. Both nations imposed their domination on the Gulf region, and from the Gulf Cup's inception in 1970 until 1990, the tournament was won by only two teams; Kuwait seven times ([[1970 Gulf Cup of Nations|1970]], [[1972 Gulf Cup of Nations|1972]], [[1974 Gulf Cup of Nations|1974]], [[1976 Gulf Cup of Nations|1976]], [[1982 Gulf Cup of Nations|1982]], [[1986 Gulf Cup of Nations|1986]], [[1990 Gulf Cup of Nations|1990]]), and despite Iraq's absence in the first three editions and withdrawal in two others, Iraq won it three times ([[1979 Gulf Cup of Nations|1979]], [[1984 Gulf Cup of Nations|1984]], [[1988 Gulf Cup of Nations|1988]]).<ref name="iraq-kuwait"/>

On 11 June 1976, the two met in the semi-final of the [[1976 AFC Asian Cup|Asian Cup]] in [[Tehran]]; Kuwait took the lead twice, and Iraq equalized twice thereafter. Finally, in the 10th minute of extra time, Kamel scored the winner for Kuwait. In 1979, the year Iraq clinched their [[1979 Gulf Cup of Nations|first Gulf Cup]] and won over Kuwait 3–1, the two met in a qualifier for the [[Football at the 1980 Summer Olympics|Moscow 1980 Olympic Games]], both managed to qualify for the Olympic Games, and both made it to the quarterfinals in [[Moscow]]. Iraq also qualified for the [[Football at the 1984 Summer Olympics|1984 Games in Los Angeles]] and the [[Football at the 1988 Summer Olympics|1988 Games in Seoul]]. The [[1982 Asian Games]] was won as well. Kuwait won the [[1980 AFC Asian Cup]], which they hosted. The nations also left their mark on the world stage. Kuwait qualified for the [[1982 FIFA World Cup|1982 World Cup]] finals in Spain. Iraq matched that in [[1986 FIFA World Cup|Mexico 1986]].<ref name="iraq-kuwait"/>

As Iraq and Kuwait traded Gulf titles in [[1988 Gulf Cup of Nations|1988]] and [[1990 Gulf Cup of Nations|1990]], few could have imagined their rivalry on the football field being replaced by an altogether more catastrophic one on the battlefield. Because of the [[Gulf War]], Iraq and Kuwait were in complete avoidance and never met for more than a decade. Kuwait's Blues had a relative recovery, winning the Gulf Cup in [[1996 Gulf Cup of Nations|1996]] and [[1998 Gulf Cup of Nations|1998]], before securing their record 10th title in [[2010 Gulf Cup of Nations|2010]]. Iraq won the [[2007 AFC Asian Cup|2007 Asian Cup]].<ref name="iraq-kuwait"/>

;Kuwait vs. Saudi Arabia
* {{flagicon|KSA}} '''[[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi Arabia]]'''

{| style="float: right; margin-left: 1em; margin-bottom: 0.5em; width: 200px; border: solid 1px"
|-
|
{| class="wikitable" style="text-align: center;font-size:90%;"
!colspan="6"|Statistics vs. {{fb|KSA}}<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kuwait - Record against Saudi Arabia |url=https://www.worldfootball.net/teams/te13986/kuwait/records-all-time-appearances/ |access-date=2023-01-11 |website=worldfootball.net |date=11 January 2023 |language=en |archive-date=2023-01-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230111011639/https://www.worldfootball.net/teams/kuwait-team/saudi-arabien-team/11/ |url-status=live }}</ref>
|-
!Played<sup>1</sup>
!Wins<sup>2</sup>
!Draws
!Losses
!GF
!GA
|-
|42||15||13||15||44||44
|}
<small>''1. Only matches recognized by [[FIFA]].''</small><br>
<small>''2. Wins for '''Kuwait'''.''</small>
|}
The football rivalry between Kuwait and [[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi Arabia]] is one of the most anticipated in the [[Persian Gulf|Gulf region]], steeped in decades of competition and regional pride. This rivalry is frequently highlighted in the [[Arabian Gulf Cup]] and other regional tournaments, attracting attention from fans and media. Kuwait achieved notable success in the 1970s and 1980s, winning the [[1980 AFC Asian Cup]] and becoming the first Arab nation to qualify for the [[FIFA World Cup 1982|FIFA World Cup in 1982]]. Key players from this golden era, such as [[Jasem Yaqoub|Jassem Yaqoub]], [[Faisal Al-Dakhil]], and [[Saad Al-Houti]], were instrumental in Kuwait's regional and continental triumphs.

Saudi Arabia saw increased success in football beginning in the late 1980s and continuing into the 1990s, with consecutive [[FIFA World Cup]] qualifications starting in [[1994 FIFA World Cup qualification|1994]] and three AFC Asian Cup victories in [[1984 AFC Asian Cup|1984]], [[1988 AFC Asian Cup|1988]], and [[1996 AFC Asian Cup|1996]]. Legendary players like [[Majed Abdullah]], who is often regarded as one of the greatest Asian footballers of all time, and [[Sami Al-Jaber]], a prolific striker with four FIFA World Cup appearances, played crucial roles in Saudi Arabia's footballing achievements.

Notable matches in this rivalry include the 1980 Arabian Gulf Cup final, where Kuwait defeated Saudi Arabia 3–1, showcasing their dominance at the time. Another significant encounter was the [[14th Arabian Gulf Cup|1998 Arabian Gulf Cup]], where Saudi Arabia emerged victorious, asserting their growing influence in regional football. The 2002 FIFA World Cup qualifiers also featured a dramatic showdown between the two teams, with Saudi Arabia securing a crucial 1–0 victory, thanks to a goal by [[Nawaf Al-Temyat]], which helped them qualify for the [[2002 FIFA World Cup|World Cup]].

=== Coaching history ===
{{div col|colwidth=25em}}
* {{flagicon|EGY|1922}} [[Ali Othman (footballer)|Ali Othman]] and [[Majid Mohammed]] (1955)
* {{flagicon|EGY|1922}} [[Ahmed Abu Taha]] (1957)
* {{flagicon|POL|1928}} [[Edmund Majowski]] (1957–1958)
* {{flagicon|YUG}} [[Ljubiša Broćić]] (1962, 1971–1973, 1973–1975)
* {{flagicon|UAR}} [[Saleh El Wahsh]] (1964)
* {{flagicon|HUN}} [[Gyula Grosics]] (1966)
* {{flagicon|YUG}} [[Dimitri Tadić]] (1966–1969)
* {{flagicon|UAR}} [[Taha El-Doukhi]] (1970)
* {{flagicon|KUW}} [[Hassan Nasser]] (1973)
* {{flagicon|BRA|1968}} [[Mário Zagallo]] (1976–1978)
* {{flagicon|KUW}} [[Saleh Zakaria]] (1978, 1986, 2006–2007)
* {{flagicon|BRA|1968}} [[Carlos Alberto Parreira]] (1978–1982)
* {{flagicon|BRA|1968}} [[Antônio Lopes]] (1983–1985)
* {{flagicon|ENG}} [[Malcolm Allison]] (1985–1986)

==Head-to-head record==
The following table shows '''Kuwait's all-time international record''', {{updated|6 June 2026}} after match against [[Thailand national football team|Thailand]]<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.worldfootball.net/teams/te13986/kuwait/records-all-time-appearances/|date=1 January 2023|access-date=11 January 2023|title=Kuwait - Record against|archive-date=11 January 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230111011641/https://www.worldfootball.net/teams/kuwait-team/11/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.11v11.com/teams/kuwait/tab/opposingTeams/ | title=Kuwait national football team: Record v other sides }}</ref>

{{legend2|#CCFFCC|Positive Record|border=1px solid #AAAAAA}}
{{legend2|#FFFFCC|Neutral Record|border=1px solid #AAAAAA}}
{{legend2|#FFCCCC|Negative Record|border=1px solid #AAAAAA}}

All friendly and international matches have been approved, '''except for Olympic matches'''.
[https://web.archive.org/web/20191026115529/https://www.fifa.com/mm/document/affederation/administration/01/79/87/75/circularno.1324-fifa-coca-colaworldranking.pdf A-level matches]

==Honours==
<!-- DO NOT ADD UNOFFICIAL OR FRIENDLY COMPETITIONS IN THE SUMMARY TABLE FOR OFFICIAL HONOURS ONLY -->

===Continental===
* '''[[AFC Asian Cup]]'''
** [[File:Coppa Asia.svg|25px]] '''Champions (1)''': [[1980 AFC Asian Cup|1980]]
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (1): [[1976 AFC Asian Cup|1976]]
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (1): [[1984 AFC Asian Cup|1984]]
* '''[[Football at the Asian Games|Asian Games]]'''<ref group=note>Competition organized by [[Olympic Council of Asia|OCA]], officially not recognized by FIFA.</ref>
** {{Silver2}} Silver medal (2): [[Football at the 1982 Asian Games|1982]], [[Football at the 1998 Asian Games|1998]]
** {{Bronze3}} Bronze medal (2): [[Football at the 1986 Asian Games|1986]], [[Football at the 1994 Asian Games|1994]]

===Regional===
* '''[[FIFA Arab Cup|Arab Cup]]'''
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (3): [[1964 Arab Cup|1964]], [[1992 Arab Cup|1992]],{{refn|group=note|The [[1992 Arab Cup]] also counted as an edition of the [[Arab Games]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Arab Cup |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesa/arabcup.html |access-date=2025-02-14 |website=[[RSSSF]]}}</ref>}} [[1998 Arab Cup|1998]]
* '''[[Arab Games]]'''
** {{Bronze3}} Bronze medal (2): [[1992 Arab Cup|1992]], [[Football at the 2011 Arab Games|2011]]
* '''[[Arabian Gulf Cup]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Champions (10)''': [[1970 Gulf Cup of Nations|1970]], [[1972 Gulf Cup of Nations|1972]], [[1974 Gulf Cup of Nations|1974]], [[1976 Gulf Cup of Nations|1976]], [[1982 Gulf Cup of Nations|1982]], [[1986 Gulf Cup of Nations|1986]], [[1990 Gulf Cup of Nations|1990]], [[1996 Gulf Cup of Nations|1996]], [[1998 Gulf Cup of Nations|1998]], [[2010 Gulf Cup of Nations|2010]]
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (1): [[1979 Gulf Cup of Nations|1979]]
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (1): [[2013 Gulf Cup of Nations|2013]], [[2002 Gulf Cup of Nations|2002]]
* '''[[West Asian Games]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Gold medal (1)''': 2002
** {{Bronze3}} Bronze medal (1): 1997
* '''[[WAFF Championship]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Champions (1)''': [[2010 WAFF Championship|2010]]
* '''[[SAFF Championship]]'''
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (1): [[2023 SAFF Championship|2023]]

===Friendly===
* '''[[2011 Focus Cup|Focus International Cup]] (1)''': 2011

===Awards===
* '''[[Arabian Gulf Cup|Arabian Gulf Cup Fair Play Award]] (1)''': [[23rd Arabian Gulf Cup|2017–18]]

===Summary===
Only official honours are included, according to ''FIFA statutes'' (competitions organized/recognized by [[FIFA]] or an affiliated confederation).

|-
